section.title: Final Greetings
section.sectionRationale: The letter closes as warmly as it began. Having warned against deceivers, the elder sets down his pen mid-thought, longing to come in person so that joy may be full, and passes on greetings from a sister church. The close returns the reader to love and joy after the stern middle.
section.thesis: The elder has much more to say but prefers to speak face to face so that shared joy may be complete, and he sends greetings from the children of the readers' elect sister, closing the letter in the warmth of the family of God.
section.frameNote: Two beats: the wish to come and speak face to face for full joy; greetings from the elect sister's children.

units.label: Face to face
units.note: that our joy may be complete

verses.text: I have many things to write to you, but I would prefer not to do so with paper and ink. Instead, I hope to come and speak with you face to face, so that our {{chara|joy}} may be complete.
The children of your {{eklekte|elect}} sister send you greetings.

chara.gloss: Joy, gladness, delight. The elder writes so that joy may be complete or made full, the same aim John names in his other letters. Christian joy is not solitary; it is completed in fellowship, in the shared life of God's people gathered face to face.

eklekte.gloss: Chosen, elect, selected by God. The readers were greeted in verse 1 as the chosen lady; now greetings come from the children of their elect sister. The word frames the whole letter, binding two communities together as families chosen by the same gracious God.

basic.context: The letter ends on a warm, personal note. The elder says he has much more to write but would rather come and talk in person, because seeing one another completes their joy. Then he passes on greetings from another church, the children of the readers' elect sister. After the sharp warning, the close returns to love and family.
basic.hermeneutics: These are ordinary closing remarks of an ancient letter, but they carry real weight. The preference for face-to-face over paper and ink shows that the truth is meant to be lived in personal fellowship, not only read. And the note about complete joy reveals the goal behind even the stern warning: the full, shared gladness of God's people.
basic.originalLanguages: The elder writes so that joy, chara, may be made full, an aim he names elsewhere too. The greeting comes from the children of the readers' elect, eklekte, sister, the same word used of the chosen lady in verse 1, framing the letter and binding the two communities as one family.
basic.typology: There is no formal type here, yet the longing to be present face to face quietly anticipates the great hope of Scripture: seeing God face to face, when joy will at last be complete in His presence.
basic.christ: The joy the elder seeks is the joy Jesus prayed His people would share fully, the joy He gives that no one can take away. The whole letter has guarded the truth about Christ; now it points to the fellowship and joy that truth secures, all of it grounded in Him.
basic.god: God is a God of joy who gathers His people into one family across distances, chosen sisters and their children. He means His truth to be enjoyed in fellowship, and He is bringing His people toward the day of complete joy in His presence.
basic.crossReferences: 3 John ends almost identically, preferring to speak face to face. 1 John says its aim is that joy may be complete. Jesus in John's Gospel speaks of His joy made full in His people.
basic.oneStory: The letter closes with the hope of presence and full joy, which is where the whole Bible story is going: God's chosen family gathered, the truth kept, and joy completed face to face. The stern warning served this end, that nothing would rob God's people of the shared gladness He is bringing them into.

context.literary: A conventional letter closing, yet crafted: the preference for presence over 'paper and ink' echoes 3 John almost word for word, and the note of complete joy ties back to 1 John. The final greeting from the elect sister's children mirrors the opening address to the chosen lady, framing the whole letter as family correspondence.
context.historical: Letters were a poor substitute for presence in the ancient world; the elder hopes to travel and teach in person. The greeting from the elect sister's children is most naturally read as a message from the elder's own congregation to the readers' community, one house church to another.
context.canonical: The close binds 2 John to 3 John, its twin, and to 1 John in aim and vocabulary. It rounds off the shortest of John's letters within the same witness that runs through his Gospel: truth, love, fellowship, and joy made full.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To signal that more remains to be said, to express a warm hope of coming in person for the sake of complete joy, and to close with greetings that bind the two communities together as one family in Christ.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The close is descriptive, the elder's own plans and greetings, rather than a command. Its abiding lesson is by example: the truth is meant to be lived in personal fellowship, and the goal of faithful teaching and warning is the full, shared joy of God's people.

debates.question: Who are the children of the elect sister in verse 13?

views.label: Another local church sending greetings
views.case: If the chosen lady of verse 1 is a congregation, then her elect sister is a sister church, and her children are its members; the elder conveys greetings from his own community to the readers'.

views.label: The nephews and nieces of a literal woman
views.case: If the chosen lady is an individual, the elect sister may be her actual sister, whose children send greetings, perhaps because that sister had died or was absent.

debates.bearing: Either reading leaves the point intact: two communities, or two households, chosen by God, greet one another as one family, and the warmth of that fellowship crowns the letter.

study.originalLanguages: The close is brief but pointed. The elder writes with an eye to joy, chara, made full or complete, the very aim he names in his first letter and the joy Jesus promised His people. He would rather this joy be completed in person than in 'paper and ink', for the truth is meant to be shared face to face. And the greeting comes from the children of the readers' elect, eklekte, sister, the same word that opened the letter with the chosen lady, so that election frames the whole and binds two communities as one chosen family.

typology.type: The longing to come and speak face to face
typology.fulfillment: The hope of seeing God face to face, when partial knowledge gives way to full sight and joy is complete, 1 Corinthians 13:12, Revelation 22:4.
The full joy Christ gives and prays for His people, consummated when His family is finally gathered, John 15:11, 17:13.

study.christ: The joy the elder longs to complete is the joy Jesus gives and prayed His people would share in full, a joy the world cannot take away. The whole letter has fought to keep the truth about Christ; here it points to the fruit of that truth, the fellowship and gladness of God's family. Christ is the one in whom that joy is found, and toward whose face-to-face presence the deepest longing of the close is finally directed.
study.god: God is a God of joy who does not leave His people scattered but gathers them into one family, chosen sisters and their children greeting one another across the miles. He means His truth to be enjoyed in real fellowship, not held in isolation, and He is carrying His people toward the day when joy will be complete in His presence. The warmth of this close reveals the heart behind even the letter's sternest warning.

crossReferences.note: An almost identical close, preferring to speak face to face rather than with pen and ink, the twin letter to this one.
John writes so that joy may be complete, the same aim behind the wish for face-to-face fellowship here.
Jesus speaks so that His joy may be in His people and their joy made full, the joy the elder seeks.
Now we see in part, but then face to face, the hope quietly echoed in the longing to be present.

study.oneStory: The letter ends where the whole Bible story is heading: God's chosen family gathered, the truth kept safe, and joy made complete face to face. The stern middle of the letter served this very end, that nothing would rob God's people of the shared gladness He is bringing them into. In Christ, truth and love and joy are all secured, and the last word is the warmth of a family that belongs to God.

sermon.title: The Danger of Deception
sermon.why: Begg's message on 2 John 1:7-13 carries through to the letter's close, treating the elder's wish to come face to face for complete joy and the greetings from the sister church. A clear, orthodox handling that reaches this final movement.

quiz.q: Why does the elder prefer not to write everything with paper and ink?

options.text: He hopes to come and speak face to face so that their joy may be complete.
options.note: Verse 12 names his preference for personal presence and its aim, full joy.

options.text: He had run out of things to say.
options.note: He says the opposite; he has many things to write.

options.text: He distrusted letters as a way to teach.
options.note: He values presence more highly, but he still writes; the point is completeness of joy, not distrust.

options.text: He wanted to save the cost of writing materials.
options.note: Paper and ink are mentioned, but the reason given is joy made complete in person, not thrift.

quiz.q: What does the elder say completes their joy?

options.text: Being together face to face in fellowship.
options.note: Verse 12 ties complete joy to personal presence among God's people.

options.text: Winning the argument against the deceivers.
options.note: The warning matters, but the joy named here is that of fellowship, not victory in dispute.

options.text: Receiving a reply letter from the readers.
options.note: He desires communion, but specifically face-to-face presence, not merely a written reply.

options.text: Being left alone to write in peace.
options.note: He longs for presence with others, not solitude.

quiz.q: Who sends greetings at the end of the letter?

options.text: The children of the readers' elect sister.
options.note: Verse 13 conveys greetings from the elect sister's children, likely a sister church.
The greeting comes from the family of God, not from the false teachers.
The greeting is from the elect sister's children, not from scribes.
The greeting is from fellow believers, the elect sister's children, not merchants.

quiz.q: How does the word elect, used of the sister, connect to the rest of the letter?

options.text: It echoes the chosen lady of verse 1, framing the letter and binding both communities as God's chosen family.
options.note: The same word opens and closes the letter, tying the two together in God's choosing.

options.text: It is an unrelated coincidence of wording.
options.note: The repetition deliberately frames the letter with the language of election.

options.text: It shows the sister church was superior to the readers.
options.note: Both are called elect equally; there is no ranking.

options.text: It refers only to a person's social rank.
options.note: The word points to God's gracious choosing, not to social standing.

questions: The elder longed to be present rather than only to write. Where in your own life is face-to-face fellowship being crowded out, and how might you recover it?
His aim was that their joy would be complete. Do you think of Christian joy as something completed together rather than alone, and how could you pursue that this week?
After a stern warning, the letter ends in warmth and family greetings. How does it help you to see that guarding the truth and deep affection belong together?
The close quietly points toward seeing God face to face. How does that final hope of complete joy in His presence shape the way you live and love now?

translationNotes.note: 'Face to face' renders a Greek idiom, literally 'mouth to mouth', as the BSB footnote notes, stressing direct personal presence.
'That our joy may be complete' can also be read 'that your joy may be complete'; manuscripts vary, and the sense of shared, full joy holds either way.
Some manuscripts add 'Amen' at the close, as the BSB footnote notes; the BSB text omits it.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 John 1:12-13. No em dashes in this passage; verse 12 'mouth to mouth' idiom and verse 13 'Amen' variant noted per the BSB footnotes.
